full visibility into tool calls, agent decisions, and error modes
. This means you don't need to build your own telemetry stack to track what your agent is doing.

For production agent workloads, you need to know:
-
Whether the agent is stuck in loops
Without built-in tracing, you'd have to build your own telemetry stack to track agent actions, token usage, and errors. With Managed Agents, the Console provides this out of the box.
Think of session tracing as a

of your agent's execution:
- The agent runs in a sandbox.
- Every action is logged as an event.
- Events are persisted server-side and can be fetched even after the session completes.
- The Console visualizes these events as a timeline you can scroll through.

You can debug in two ways:
-
: Fetch raw events via API and analyze them in code.
The Console provides a visual timeline of your agent sessions. Navigate to the
: All sessions with status, creation time, model used.
: Time-ordered view of events (content, timestamps, token usage) within a session. Only developers and admins can access this.
: Details for each tool call and its result.
: Track token consumption to optimize prompts and reduce costs.
For programmatic debugging, retrieve raw events via API:

events = client.beta.sessions.events.list(session.id)
for event in events:
    print(f"Type: {event.type}")
    print(f"Processed at: {event.processed_at}")
    
    match event.type:
        case "user.message" | "agent.message":
            for block in event.content:
                print(f"Block: {block.type}")
                if block.type == "text":
                    print(f"Text: {block.text[:100]}...")
        
        case "agent.tool_use" | "agent.custom_tool_use" | "agent.mcp_tool_use":
            print(f"Tool: {event.name}")
            print("---")
Use the same flow to display errors and track token consumption:

session.error  # Check for session errors

events = client.beta.sessions.events.list(session.id)
input_tokens, output_tokens = 0, 0
for event in events:
    match event.type:
        case "session.error":
            print(f"[{event.error.type}] {event.error.message}")
        case "span.model_request_end":
            input_tokens += event.model_usage.input_tokens
            output_tokens += event.model_usage.output_tokens
            print(f"Total input tokens: {input_tokens}, output tokens: {output_tokens}")
This gives you full control over how you analyze and visualize session data.
Communication with Managed Agents is event-based. Events flow in two directions:
: Kick off sessions and steer them as they progress.
: Updates the agent's system prompt between turns.
timestamp indicating when it was recorded server-side.
: Model request completions with token usage.
each tool call and its result
in detail. This is critical for debugging unexpected agent behavior, as tool failures often explain why the agent does something wrong.

For example:
- Which tool was called.
- What arguments were passed.
- What the tool returned.
- Whether the tool failed.

events. You can:
- Fetch the error type and message.
- Understand the failure mode.
- Use this information to fix the agent's configuration or prompt.
to optimize prompts and reduce costs:

events = client.beta.sessions.events.list(session.id)
input_tokens, output_tokens = 0, 0
for event in events:
    match event.type:
        case "span.model_request_end":
            input_tokens += event.model_usage.input_tokens
            output_tokens += event.model_usage.output_tokens
The session object also includes a
field with cumulative token statistics. You can fetch the session after it goes idle to read the latest totals for tracking costs or enforcing budgets.
You can add logging instructions to the system prompt so the agent
. This helps you understand why the agent made certain decisions.

Always explain your reasoning before and after each tool call.
Be explicit about what you're trying to achieve.

For quick debugging:
1. Open the session in the Console.
2. Scroll through the timeline to see the sequence of events.
3. Inspect any tool call or error.
4. Look for patterns (e.g., repeated tool failures, stuck loops).
This is the fastest way to understand what happened without writing code.

For deeper analysis or custom dashboards:
1. Use the API to fetch all events for a session.
2. Analyze tool calls, errors, and token usage.
3. Aggregate data across multiple sessions for trends.
4. Build custom reports or alerts.
This is useful for production workloads where you need custom observability.
Add logging to the system prompt
By adding logging instructions to the system prompt, you can make the agent more transparent about its decisions. This complements the Console's event tracing.
Session tracing is part of a broader

for understanding how agents interact with external systems.
-
for inspecting tool calls, agent decisions, and failure modes.

This means you can:
- See what tools are called and why.
- Understand integration failures.
- Track costs and optimize token usage.
- Audit agent decisions for compliance.

- No need to build your own telemetry stack.
- Full audit trail of every agent action.
- Visual timeline for quick debugging.
- Raw event API for programmatic analysis.

- Tracks token usage and costs.
- Detects errors and failure modes.
- Supports compliance and audit requirements.

- Built-in observability without custom infrastructure.
- Full event history persisted server-side.
- Console timeline for quick debugging.
- API access to raw events for custom analysis.

- Sessions persist data server-side, so you lose Zero Data Retention and HIPAA BAA eligibility.
- You're using the Console and API's observability features rather than fully custom tools.
- Event history is available until the session is deleted, but checkpoints are only preserved for 30 days.
full visibility into every agent action
includes token tracking, error detection, tool execution details, and agent decision logs.
Every action is logged as an event with timestamps, tool calls, inputs, outputs, and errors.
You don't need to build your own telemetry stack; the Console provides this out of the box.
Understanding the internals of session tracing and run observability helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with session tracing and run observability has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying session tracing and run observability in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Session tracing and run observability is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
